prompt:
Research query: What are the proven health benefits and risks of intermittent fasting in adults with metabolic syndrome?

Below are evidence statements from a literature review. Extract ALL named studies, trials, and author references mentioned.

EVIDENCE:
- Secretome alterations are linked to health complications associated with weight regain after weight loss.
- TRE reduced fasting insulin by MD -0.81 vs non-intervention diet across 14 studies (I-squared 94.0%).
- In patients with metabolic syndrome, TWF/MADF improved 13 metabolic and anthropometric outcomes vs CER/RD across 4-6 trials.
- TRE 7-9 h reduced fasting glucose by MD -2.82 mg/dL vs unrestricted diet (4 studies, I-squared 0%).
- Changes in amino-acid, fat and carbohydrate metabolites over time may modulate circadian rhythm, ghrelin, insulin resistance and autophagy.
- The study set out to characterise changes in metabolite concentration over time under intermittent feeding.
- The Intermittent versus Continuous Feeding in Critically Ill Patients (NC...) study aimed to characterise these metabolite changes.
- Ramadan fasting and its attendant lifestyle changes induce changes in circadian rhythm and associated physiological and metabolic functions.
- Prior studies on psychomotor performance during Ramadan fasting have reported conflicting results.
- The study used a case-crossover design to objectively assess effects of intermittent fasting on drowsiness, blink total duration, and mean reaction time both during and outside Ramadan to control for lifestyle changes.
- Intermittent fasting produced a mean weight reduction of 3.59 kg (95% CI: -4.59 to -2.59, p < 0.0001) in metabolic syndrome populations.
- Intermittent fasting reduced systolic blood pressure by 5.54 mmHg (95% CI: -7.55 to -3.53, p < 0.0001) in metabolic syndrome patients.
- Intermittent fasting reduced LDL cholesterol by 56.22 mg/dL (95% CI: -80.14 to -32.29, p < 0.0001) in adults with metabolic syndrome.
- Treatment of massively obese patients via extended or intermittent starvation has historically been described as a relatively benign method for initiating weight reduction.
- Intermittent fasting protocols including 16/8 and 5:2 aid weight management and improve insulin sensitivity.
- Intermittent fasting enhances insulin sensitivity, reduces caloric intake, and promotes fat oxidation, producing significant weight loss and improved blood glucose.
- Detox diets including IF are notable strategies for managing obesity and metabolic syndrome alongside ketogenic, Paleo, vegetarian, Mediterranean, DASH, and Whole30 approaches.
- IF intervention reduced body weight by WMD -1.78 kg (95% CI -2.21 to -1.35) compared to control group diets in pooled RCTs.
- In a mouse model of sleep-apnea intermittent hypoxia, IH lowered fasting glucose and insulin in males but impaired glucose tolerance in ovariectomized females.
- Ten RCTs with 701 individuals were pooled to evaluate IF effects on glycemic control, lipid profile, and inflammatory markers.
- IF reduced fasting blood sugar with pooled SMD = -0.51 (95% CI: -0.81, -0.20; p = 0.001).
- IF reduced fasting insulin with pooled SMD = -0.27 (95% CI: -0.52, -0.03; p = 0.027).
- IF, time-restricted eating, and fasting-mimicking diets have emerged as weight loss programs contributing to improved cardiometabolic health.
- Participants were instructed to maintain a 10:14 intermittent fasting regimen daily for 6 months.
- Lifestyle and dietary modification are central to achieving effective weight loss in obesity.
- Very low calorie ketogenic diets (VLCKD) can produce sustained weight loss and in some individuals remission of type 2 diabetes.
- Medical nutrition therapy delivered by trained registered dietitian nutritionists is clinically beneficial and cost-effective for cardiometabolic disease.
- General dietary recommendations include limiting highly processed foods, fatty meats, and added sugars; Mediterranean and DASH diets are the two most researched accepted healthy patterns.
- Intermittent fasting promotes healthy adipose tissue remodeling and has emerged as an effective strategy to combat obesity and improve metabolic homeostasis.
- IF-mediated metabolic adaptations involve key signalling pathways including PI3K, SIRT, mTOR, and AMPK.
- Dysfunctional adipose tissue expansion from prolonged calorie surplus produces rapid adipocyte enlargement, hypoxia, inflammation, and insulin resistance.
- Intermittent fasting promotes interleukin-22 production by type 3 innate lymphoid cells (ILC3s) and subsequent beigeing of subcutaneous white adipose tissue.
- The global intermittent fasting app market size was USD 0.59 billion in 2026, projected to grow to USD 1.22 billion by 2035 at a CAGR of 8.4%.
- IF apps are informatics tools that facilitate users in managing fasting schedules and adopting a healthy lifestyle, with features including flexible scheduling, alarms, and wearable/health-tracker syncing.
- This Frontiers in Nutrition 2025 systematic review and meta-analysis aimed to evaluate IF effects on glycemic control, lipid profile, and inflammatory markers in adults with metabolic syndrome using Cochrane RoB2 and GRADE evidence grading.
- Periodic Fasting (PF) involves water-only fasting or FMD for at least two consecutive days repeated monthly, with maximum daily energy intake of 250 kcal for about one week.
- The Fasting-Mimicking Diet is a five-day regimen at 25% of usual food intake and less than 10% of protein, centered on healthful fats and fiber-rich carbohydrates.
- Time-restricted feeding (TRF) involves daily no/restricted energy intake for 12-20h with an eating window of 4-12h, producing at least a 20% caloric reduction.
- Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease is associated with substantial global liver-related and cardiovascular-related morbidity and mortality.
- The timing of energy intake across the day is an important determinant of individual risk for NAFLD and associated metabolic conditions.
- High total energy intake with unhealthy ultra-processed foods and saturated fats has long been regarded as a major dietary driver of NAFLD.
- Olive oil provides gut and cardiovascular protection during refeeding phases of intermittent fasting.

For each named study, output ONE line in this format:
STUDY: <first_author_last_name> | <year_if_known> | <short_description>

Examples:
STUDY: Trepanowski | 2017 | 12-month ADF vs daily calorie restriction RCT
STUDY: Varady | 2022 | meta-analysis of alternate-day fasting protocols
STUDY: Longo | 2016 | fasting-mimicking diet clinical trial

Output ONLY the STUDY lines. If no named studies are found, output: NONE
